---
title: "Elliptic Fourier Analysis"
description: "Theory of elliptic Fourier analysis and implementation in R."
date: "2023-03-06"
date-modified: "2023-03-06"
draft: true
lang: en
categories: [r]
---

::: {.callout-note}
Original Japanese version: [楕円フーリエ解析](../../../posts/2023-03-06-elliptic-fourier-analysis/index.qmd)
:::

This is a note from theory to implementation for elliptic Fourier analysis, a method for analyzing contour shapes.

## What Is Elliptic Fourier Analysis?

Elliptic Fourier analysis is a method used to analyze contours.
It is used in morphometrics.

It can quantify and visualize questions such as:

- how similar contours are among samples
- what kinds of differences exist among sample contour shapes

Examples of studies using this method include the following.
This page attempts to explain the flow from theory to R implementation and analysis as simply as possible.

## Chain Code

The direction and starting point differ slightly across references, but a direction associated with a number is called a chain, and a vector formed by arranging those numbers is called a chain code.

$$
V = a_1 a_2 a_3 \cdots a_K
$$

Here, $$V$$ is the chain code, and $$ a_1 a_2 a_3 \cdots a_K $$ are the individual chains.
It seems that Freeman (1974) was probably the first to propose this.
Arrows are extended counterclockwise every 45 degrees from 0 degrees to 360 degrees.
Because there are eight arrows, numbers from 0 to 7 are assigned to them.
It looks like this.

<div id="chaincode" style="width:600px;height:600px;"></div>
<script src="{{ site.baseurl }}/assets/js/chaincode.js"></script>

Using a simple example, represent a contour shape with chain code.

Here, consider a simple octagon that includes all directions.

The chain code is represented as follows.

$$
V = 07654321
$$

The figure is as follows.
The starting point is basically the upper left.

<div id="chaincode_ex" style="width:600px;height:600px;"></div>
<script src="{{ site.baseurl }}/assets/js/chaincode_ex.js"></script>

Note that this octagon is not a regular octagon.

That is, not all sides have the same length.

When moving vertically or horizontally, the length is $$1$$, but when moving diagonally, the length is $$\sqrt{2}$$.

The correspondence between each chain, the displacement in the $$x$$ and $$y$$ coordinates, and the length is shown in the table below.

<style>
table {
 border-collapse: collapse; /* show adjacent borders as one */
 border-spacing: 0; /* remove spacing between borders */
 text-align: center;
}
table th {
 background: #e3f6f5;
 border: solid 1px #666666;
 padding: 10px;
}
table td {
 border: solid 1px #666666;
 padding: 10px;
}
</style>
| Chain | $$x$$ displacement | $$y$$ displacement | Length         |
| ---- | --------- | --------- | ------------ |
| 0    | 1         | 0         | 1            |
| 1    | 1         | 1         | $$\sqrt{2}$$ |
| 2    | 0         | 1         | 1            |
| 3    | -1        | 1         | $$\sqrt{2}$$ |
| 4    | -1        | 0         | 1            |
| 5    | -1        | -1        | $$\sqrt{2}$$ |
| 6    | 0         | -1        | 1            |
| 7    | 1         | -1        | $$\sqrt{2}$$ |

Think of the length, or magnitude, of a single arrow as corresponding to the time needed to trace the contour.
For example, arrows 0, 2, 4, and 6 take time 1, while arrows 1, 3, 5, and 7 take time $$\sqrt{2}$$.
Therefore, the time $$t_p$$ required to trace up to the $$p$$th chain is expressed as follows.

$$
t_p = \sum^p_{i=1} \Delta t_i
$$

Here, $$\Delta t_i$$ is the time required to trace one arrow.
The displacements in the $$x$$ and $$y$$ coordinates up to the $$p$$th chain are expressed as follows.

$$
x_p = \sum^p_{i=1} \Delta x_i
$$

$$
y_p = \sum^p_{i=1} \Delta y_i
$$

## Fourier Series Expansion of a Contour

From here, the discussion turns to Fourier series expansion and related topics.
This page omits the basic theory of Fourier series expansion and Fourier transforms.
The final goal of this page is contour analysis, so if this section is difficult, it is fine to skip ahead.

The Fourier series expansion for the $$x$$ coordinate is expressed as follows.

$$
x(t) = A_0 + \sum^\infty_{n=1} a_n \cos \frac{2n \pi t}{T} + b_n \sin \frac{2n \pi t}{T}
$$

Here, $$T$$ is the period, which can be interpreted as the tracing time for a contour shape.
$$A_0, a_n, b_n$$ are expressed as follows.

$$
A_0 =
$$

## Calculating Elliptic Fourier Coefficients

## Explanatory Power

## Standardization

$$
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
a_n^{**} & b_n^{**} \\
c_n^{**} & d_n^{**} \\
\end{array}
\right]
=
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
\cos \psi & \sin \psi \\
-\sin \psi & \cos \psi \\
\end{array}
\right]
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
a_n & b_n \\
c_n & d_n \\
\end{array}
\right]
\left[
\begin{array}{rr}
\cos \frac{2 \pi n t}{T} &  -\sin \frac{2 \pi n t}{T} \\
\sin \frac{2 \pi n t}{T} &\cos \frac{2 \pi n t}{T} \\
\end{array}
\right]
$$

## Principal Component Analysis

## Inverse Fourier Transform
